plan:
- ESP32-WROOM-32 microcontroller, L298N motor driver, SSD1306 OLED display, 2x 18650 Li-ion batteries (7.4V nominal).
- Four BO DC motors for differential drive, connected to the L298N.
- Robot chassis/frame, four wheels, and necessary mounting hardware for components.
- Custom 3D-printed brackets for ESP32, L298N, OLED, and battery holder integration.
- Wiring harnesses for power distribution, motor control, and I2C communication lines.

alternatives.name: LiPo (Lithium Polymer) Battery
alternatives.reason: Offers greater flexibility in form factor and potentially higher discharge rates for specific applications, though often more delicate.

alternatives.name: AA/AAA NiMH Rechargeable Batteries
alternatives.reason: Lower voltage (1.2V per cell) and generally lower energy density, but widely available and often safer with simpler charging requirements.

alternatives.name: Larger capacity 21700 Li-ion Battery
alternatives.reason: Similar chemistry and form factor to 18650 but offers higher capacity and often higher discharge rates due to its larger size (21mm diameter, 70mm length).

originalPrompt:
Create a production-quality Arduino project called "Nico V2", a smart Wi-Fi controlled robot built using an ESP32 Dev Module.

Project Information
-------------------
Project Name: Nico OS
Creators: Zahid Khan & Indra Vamsi
App Name: ZAHID'sFirst

Objective
---------
Build a modular, expandable, and professional ESP32 robot firmware instead of a single Arduino sketch. The code should be clean, object-oriented where appropriate, heavily commented, and easy to maintain.

Hardware
--------
• ESP32 Dev Module (ESP32-WROOM-32)
• L298N Motor Driver
• Four BO DC Motors (2 motors on each side wired in parallel)
• Two 18650 Li-ion batteries connected in series (7.4V nominal)
• 0.91"/0.96" I2C OLED Display (SSD1306, 128x64)
• Future support for VL53L0X distance sensor

Pin Configuration
-----------------
Motor Driver
ENA -> GPIO14
IN1 -> GPIO27
IN2 -> GPIO26
IN3 -> GPIO25
IN4 -> GPIO33
ENB -> GPIO32

OLED
SDA -> GPIO21
SCL -> GPIO22

Communication
-------------
Use WiFi UDP.

Robot listens on UDP port 12345.

Connect to WiFi hotspot using configurable SSID and Password.

Automatically reconnect to WiFi if disconnected.

Display connection status on Serial Monitor and OLED.

Command Protocol
----------------
Support both:

F
B
L
R
S

and

f,150
b,150
l,120
r,120
s,0

The parser should automatically detect the command format.

Speed range:
0–255

Robot Features
--------------
Forward

Backward

Left

Right

Stop

Automatic stop if no command is received for 200 milliseconds.

PWM speed control.

Emergency stop.

Software Requirements
---------------------
Create a professional folder structure.

Nico_V2/

Nico_V2.ino

Config.h

Motor.h

Motor.cpp

Display.h

Display.cpp

WiFiControl.h

WiFiControl.cpp

CommandParser.h

CommandParser.cpp

Logo.h

README.md

libraries.txt

Architecture
------------
Use classes.

Motor class

Display class

WiFiControl class

CommandParser class

Avoid global variables wherever possible.

No delay() inside loop().

Use millis() timers.

OLED Interface
--------------
Startup animation.

Display:

NICO

Creators:
Zahid Khan
Indra

Then show:

WiFi Status

IP Address

Direction

Speed

Battery placeholder

If WiFi disconnects:

Display:
WiFi Lost
Reconnecting...

Serial Monitor
--------------
Display:

===================================
NICO V2

Creators:
Zahid Khan
&
Indra

Connecting WiFi...

Connected

IP Address

Listening UDP

Robot Ready
===================================

Motor Control
-------------
Use ESP32 LEDC PWM.

5000 Hz

8-bit resolution.

Smooth acceleration and deceleration.

Maintain equal speed on left and right motors.

Code Quality
------------
Modern C++.

Extensively commented.

Modular.

GitHub-ready.

No deprecated ESP32 functions.

Future Expansion
----------------
Design the project so these modules can later be added without changing the architecture:

VL53L0X obstacle avoidance

Battery voltage monitoring

ESP32-CAM

Voice commands

RGB LEDs

Buzzer

Autonomous navigation

Web dashboard

OTA firmware updates

Deliverables
------------
Generate all source files completely.

Include README with wiring diagram, installation instructions, library requirements, upload instructions, troubleshooting guide, and project architecture explanation.

The final project should look like a polished open-source robotics repository created by professional embedded software engineers.
projectDescription: Nico V2 Robot is an ESP32-powered, Wi-Fi controlled mobile platform utilizing an L298N motor driver for four BO DC motors. It features an OLED display for status and is powered by series-connected 18650 batteries, all integrated onto a modular chassis.

Project idea: Create a production-quality Arduino project called "Nico V2", a smart Wi-Fi controlled robot built using an ESP32 Dev Module.

Project Information
-------------------
Project Name: Nico OS
Creators: Zahid Khan & Indra Vamsi
App Name: ZAHID'sFirst

Objective
---------
Build a modular, expandable, and professional ESP32 robot firmware instead of a single Arduino sketch. The code should be clean, object-oriented where appropriate, heavily commented, and easy to maintain.

Hardware
--------
• ESP32 Dev Module (ESP32-WROOM-32)
• L298N Motor Driver
• Four BO DC Motors (2 motors on each side wired in parallel)
• Two 18650 Li-ion batteries connected in series (7.4V nominal)
• 0.91"/0.96" I2C OLED Display (SSD1306, 128x64)
• Future support for VL53L0X distance sensor

-----------------
Motor Driver
ENA -> GPIO14
IN1 -> GPIO27
IN2 -> GPIO26
IN3 -> GPIO25
IN4 -> GPIO33
ENB -> GPIO32

SDA -> GPIO21
SCL -> GPIO22

-------------

Robot listens on UDP port 12345.

Automatically reconnect to WiFi if disconnected.

Display connection status on Serial Monitor and OLED.

The parser should automatically detect the command format.

--------------

---------------------
Create a professional folder structure.

Deliverables

Generate all source files completely.

Include README with wiring diagram, installation instructions, library requirements, upload instructions, troubleshooting guide, and project architecture explanation.

The final project should look like a polished open-source robotics repository created by professional embedded software engineers.

Design notes: Modular architecture, Expandable design, Professional grade, Clean code, Object-oriented, GitHub-ready

Electrical components (current): Main Controller (mcu, 55x28x13mm); Motor Driver (module, 43x43x27mm); 4x actuator: Left Front DC Motor, Left Rear DC Motor, Right Front DC Motor, Right Rear DC Motor; 2x power: Battery 1, Battery 2; OLED Display (display, 30x30x4mm)

Mechanical/structural parts (current): Chassis Base Plate (structural, 150x100x3mm); 4x mechanism: Left Front Wheel, Right Front Wheel, Left Rear Wheel, Right Rear Wheel; 3x misc: Chassis Mounting Screws, M3 Nuts, M3 Brass Standoffs; 8x 3d_printed: ESP32 Dev Module Mount, L298N Motor Driver Mount, OLED Display Mount, 18650 Series Battery Holder +4 more

Assembly: Left Front BO Motor Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); Right Front BO Motor Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); Left Rear BO Motor Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); Right Rear BO Motor Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); ESP32 Dev Module Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); L298N Motor Driver Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); OLED Display Mount → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); 18650 Series Battery Holder → Chassis Base Plate (M3 bolts and nuts); ESP32 Dev Module Mount → Main Controller (mount); L298N Motor Driver Mount → Motor Driver (mount); OLED Display Mount → OLED Display (mount); 18650 Series Battery Holder → Battery 1 (mount); Left Front BO Motor Mount → Left Front DC Motor (mount); Right Front BO Motor Mount → Right Front DC Motor (mount); Left Rear BO Motor Mount → Left Rear DC Motor (mount) (+1 more connections)
